CURRENT AFFAIRS,

Mr. John Redmond devoted his speech at Mary-
Mr.
Redmond is an eloquent advocate when others supply
the brief. He is not to be blamed for his lack of indi-
vidual insight and force, and he carries himself modestly
and puts up for no more than he is. As chairman of a
strong party it would be hard to find his equal; unfor-
tunately, the party is not strong, and to add to Mits

T.

anything unnecessarily to his: difficulties.

The Parliamentarians are waking up to the Industrial
ival, but they don ppear to have their heart in -

t—and more’s the pity. Mr. Redmond’s speech

ryborough was rather flat; he has done his. best,

_industries is going on to this moment.”

no doubt, with the industrial instructions on his brief,
but the Industrial Revival evidently does not interest
him; he has taken it up in‘a perfunctory sort of a
manner as part of the new programme that is intended
to cover the Council Bill debacle. Six years ago Mr.
John Redmond spoke on industry at Lismore, and we
wrote an article on part of that speech, which article
we entitled “ Raimeis up to Date.”” On the occasion
we refer to, Mr. Redmond stated: ‘‘ The towns and
villages depend upon the land; the farmer, the arti-
zan, and the labourer all depend upon the land, and
nothing can improve the material condition and the in-
dustrial condition of our country until the industry of
the land has been freed from the shackles that to-day
are bound around it by the cursed system of English
landlordism, which was implanted in this country by
our enemies.””  Mr. John Redmond, no doubt, was
speaking according to his orthodox political brief at
that time; these were the days of ¢ unity,”” when Mr.
O’Brien had recently extracted the ‘ poisoned bullet,’”
Tim Healy. Commenting on this nation-killing doctrine
propounded in those days of ‘‘ unity,” we remarked at
the time:—*“Ts it then because of the unsettled state
of the Land Question that the people are compelled to
wear Northampton boots, though they could get equal
value in the home-manufactured article. 5 o
Because of the land men wash themselves in English
soap; because of the land so many of our colleges turn
out people fit to administer the affairs of India and
other parts, but unfit to develop Ireland—for developing
Ireland frequently means importing foreigners to show
the way; because of the land the coons amuse us, and
England supplies us with ‘ drama ’; because of the land
we are nearly all speaking English, and import tons of
English periodicals ; because of the land we pay thirteen,
or is it fourteen, millions sterling for drink ; because of
the land foreigners do our insurance work, and fill so
many of the best commercial and industrial positions;
perhaps even the Land Question explains the presence
of our truly reptiles; and Belfast, for all we know, may
have grown out of the land.”

That was six years ago in the days of ““ unity *’ before
Wm. C’Brien broke away and went over to the other
side of the street to shout ‘ unity,”” with a view to
dragging the others after him. Neither then or since
then have the Parliamentarians taken the industrial
revival in earnest. A great deal of water has rum
under the bridges since then ; the industrial revival that -
we started has grown big, comparatively, and the Par-
liamentarians have come on such hard and troubled
times that no one thanks them for taking up, or pre-
tending to take up, the industrial question now. For
our part, all we regret is that even now they don’t put
their hearts into it.

Mr. John Redmond at Maryborough had a lot to say
about the killing of our industries. by England. Good-
ness knows, the time has come when all that wijght be
taken as granted and agreed at public demonstrations.
Mr. Redmond said that from the time of the Union
‘“ industrial pursuits from that day to this have gome
down, and they are going down at this. moment.””  We
very ‘much doubt the accuracy of the last part of that
statement. Since we started seven years ago we believe
that Irish industry has been picking up. Mr. Redmond
quoted an extract from the Census Report of 1901,
which showed that certain classes of workers had

decreased in Ireland in comparison witk previous
.decades, and Mr. Redmond, with quite an extraordinary
disregard for logic, says—‘‘ Therefore, the decay of Irish
Therefore

nothing of the sort necessarily follows; and, as a matter

~ of fact, we believe that Irish industrial matters are very
much on the mend since 1901. Mr. Redmond and
others like him are too crusted in the ways of thinking
that obtained in the old times when politics and nationa-
lity“ were convertible terms. We fear that they can
only respond partially, and in a half-hearted way at
that, to the new Ireland that is growing at the present
time. Tf we see no case for withdrawing the Party from
Parliament, it must not be forgotten that the Parlia-
mentary Party is only a wheel in the national machine,
and by no means one of the big wheels eitker. M.

_ Redmond said :—‘ Well, now, my own personal convic-
‘tion is, that you will never have a really successful in-
dustrial development in Ireland, you will never really
stop emigration in Ireland by any means except under
the fostering care of a native Parliament and a native
Government.  But, fellow-countrymen, that is no
reason why we should not do our best. That is no
reason why we should fold our arms and do nothing.
On the contrary, it is our duty to press upon the Govern-
ment of the day, by exposure on the floor of the House
of Commons, by votes in tle Division Lobby, by appeals
to the masses of the English working men—to press upon
the Government of the day those steps which we con-
sider they can take to improve the industrial condition
of Ireland. And, besides, we should do, individually
and collectively, all that it is in our power to do to
revive Irish industries; and believe me if we all act
together we can do something.””  If the people who
made the Industrial Revival such as it is during the past
seven years waited until Mr. Redmond and his friends
got ITome Rule for Ireland, they would have a long
wait indeed. Exposure on ‘‘the floor,” votes in the
Division Lobby, and appeals to the English workingmen
come first, and as a sort of an after-thought, a mere
‘“ besides,”” comes a reference to what the people can do
themselves! And what, pray, is to be the nature of
the appeal to the British working classes in favour of
Trisk industry! Certainly these remarks of Mr. John
Redmond on the industrial situation do not set our
blood afire with enthusiasm. We are glad that the
United Irish League authorities have seen the need of
doing something in the Irish Industrial propaganda,
but we yet have to be convinced that they have their
hearts in it.

An Irishman at present living in a very populous

part of England, and who tells us that he has taken the
Leaper for over six years, writes us for information
‘“as to the best way to get in touch with the best
makers of Irish goods.” He adds:—‘‘ We have a good
number of Irishmen in , and we want, if pos-
sible, to push Irish goods by inducing shopkeepers to
stock and pusk them. Failing this, we shall, if at all
possible, start an agency ourselves. Whichever method
is adopted we must, to be successful, get in touch with
the best houses both. for cheapness and quality, for we
can only hope to introduce such goods as will compete
in quality and price with those already offered to the
public. T would therefore ask you to kindly put us in
communication with the heads of the various associa-
‘tions that have already been formed to develop the in-
dustrial revival. T need hardly say it would be quite
impossible to read the Leaper week after week for six
years without becoming possessed with its spirit and
enthusiasm for Irish Ireland, and I take this opportu-
_nity of thanking you ‘most sincerely for the personal
pleasure you have afforded me, in seeing the great work
the LEaDER has accomplished for the cause of Ireland’s
industrial progress. I can only re-echo the hope of
many that before very long a Daily LeADER may be an
accomplisked fact.””

Our correspondent, could scarcely do better than com-
municate with our advertisers. ~We have all along

stood oub for placing the industrial revival on the broad :

basis that the producers must put goods on the market
that can stand the test of quality and price. In our
circumstances it is the only sane and ‘practical general
principle to go upon. Others whose hearts do them

THE LEADER.

OcToBER 19, 1907.

more credit than their heads have talked of reviving
Trisk.industry by’ calling upon the people to pay fancy
prices for goods because the shopman tells them—and
some shopmen say more than their prayers-even in Tre-
land—that they were Irish. ~Others again have gone
on this line because their instinct is always to play to
the gallery, and get the cheers of tke unthinking mob
on their side. Tkis sort of talk has already done a great
deal of grievous harm and no good. Now, in view of the
principle that we have set up concerning the attitude
of the consumer towards the industrial revival, we do nqb
think our correspondent could do betiter than communi-
cate with the Irish manufacturers who advertise their

goods in the LEADER.

et our correspondent and his friends take up a few
copies of the LeapEr, and they will find a fine array of
Trisk: manufacturers. If they want tobacco they can
write to Goodbody’s, to Carroll and Co., Dundal}:; to
Gallaher’s, to Murray’s; for starch, they can write to
Silverspring Starch Co.; for cloth, they have Dripsey,
Co. Cork; Foxford, Co. Mayo; Dinsmore, Ballymena,
and the Kilkenny Woollen Mills ; for furniture, the Kil-
kenny Wood Works and Messrs. .O’Dea & Co., Dublin;
for poplin ties, Elliott’s, of Dublin, and so on. If our
correspondent and his friends mention the LEADER in
their letters, we feel sure that the various firms will, if
there is margin for improvement in the matter, give
them special attention.

As real interest in politics dwindles owing to the
pottiness and squabbling of tke politicians and policy
mongers, the reports of speeches of politicians appear to
expand. Who reads the reports of all these speeches?
We don’t. And we doubt if anyone else in the country
does. What a lack of judgment the ‘“ Dwindler ” dis-
plays in thinking, as it apparently does, that the way to
meet ha’penny and other competition is to shovel in
more speeches. In Monday’s < Freeman’ we counted
about 13} columns devoted to political meetings and
speeches!” No wonder the ‘“ Freeman’s’’ dividends are
dwindling. When we Lave a daily LEaDER the orators
will have their raimeis well clipped, and orders will be
given that ‘‘ great speeches’ are not to be reported
unless in so far as there may be something in them—
which probably will be seldom.

Mr. Seumas MacManus las spun a yarn in the
““ North American Review ’> which perhaps the Yanks
may swallow. The Yanks are a very gullible people.
Recently the effects of a mere scientific training were
discussed ; perhaps story-writers get in the habit of
believing their own yarns, and Mr. MacManus has been
writing a great many yarns—some say with a tinge of
stage Irishism.  Mr. MacManus treated the Yanks
to a dream. It ‘appears that even the men
who built creameries and improved the breed of hens
with other “ various units ’’ gravitated together, ‘‘ and,
less than two years ago, under the guidance of a Dublin
boy named Arthur Griffith, they elected a National
Council, and formed themselves into a party khown as
the ¢ Sinn Fein Party,” which included probably three-
fourths of the national thinkers in Ireland.”” Verily, it
would take a Yank himself to beat that yarn! So the
poultry reformer and tlhe creamery men are tucked
under the folds of the ‘‘National Council.”” The
dominion of Edward VIIT. of Kildare Street Club is
growing—in print, anyway !

Mr. MacManus tucks up his sleeves, and leathers .

away at the Parliamentarians with highly inflated
rhetoric, but we are not particularly interested in that;
let one of the Party take him up if he wishes. In the
ccurse of Mr. MacManus’s comic story we find that the
Sweetmanites are, amongst other things, to, ‘“as far as
possible,’’ resist actively and passively all British levied
taxes. The Yanks are not told that Mr. Sweetman has

‘refused to passively resist the income tax man for fear

his cups and saucers and things might be seized!

We deal with General Sir William Butler’s address
to the Catholic Truth Society of Ireland in an article in
another part of the paper. In the course of the remarks
ou the address His Grace the Axchbishop of  Tuam,
Most Rev. Dr. Healy said:—“I am sorry to see so
much of the daily newspapers devoted to recounting the
ackievements of horses at these race meetings—nob
merely a column or two, but you will see, as everyone
knows, the whole page devoted to the various meetings
ia England and Ireland, but not in Scotland—the
Scottish are too wise. I dare say no prudent man in
business would continue to employ in his office or other-
wise a man that he knew was a gambler.”” Little heed
will the ‘“ Freeman’’ or the ‘“Independent’’ pay to
these remarks of His Grace!

There was a striking contrast between the lecturer of
the evening and one of the speakers—Sergeant Charles
O’Connor, K.C. Sergeant O’Connor, a Trinity College
Cawtholic, droned out a few grammatical nothings, and
We certainly were not surprised at his non-
descript performance; what we were surprised at was
that he was asked to speak at all. Catholics are advised
not to go to Trinity College, and we had thought that
the time had come at last when Trinity Cawtholics would
b2 treated as blacklegs; or if the old ones were partly
acquitted on the plea of the state of public opinion in
their time that at least they would be kept in the back-
ground, and not trotted out as speakers at great Catho-
lic public meetings. What did this Trinity Cawtholic
ever do in the fighting line for Irish Catholics? Perhaps
in twenty years’ time Nutting scholar blacklegs will be

" orating on lay Catholic platforms, whilst the men who

refused to enter the Souper University will be un-
horoured in the back seats. There is a want of
thoroughness about the national attitude which is a
source of weakness in the fight against the Trinity in-
justice and monopoly. There are so many lay Catholic
platforms champions who snapped their fingers early in
life at the Hierarcky, and went into Trinity! If we
had our way we would—unless they showed and ex-
pressed repentance and asked forgiveness—so far as lay
Catholic- platforms are concerned, keep them in
Coventry. ;

General Butler said:— The.Green Isle may be a
beautiful idea in poetry and song, but it is a sorrowful
fact in the life of a nation. Grass such as we sec in Ire-
land spells decay ; it is emblematic of death ; it flourishes
ia the shadow of the ruin, and it grows rankest in the
graveyard.”” Might we add that the grass that General
Butler spoke of is also fittingly emblematic of tle green
people who now so largely inhabit the land ?

Altogether the Catholic Truth Conference was, this
year, a great success. Unfortunately, we were not able
to be present at the paper on ‘‘ Scientific Training and
Religious Belief,”” by Father John; ‘“ Voices of Irish
History,” by Mr., T. B.' Cronin, or “ Tuberculosis—a
National Danger,”” by Professor McWeeney.  The

' THE LEADER. e

reception this year by His Grace the Archbishop of
Tuam was, not only a very pleasant, but important and
useful function. Last year it will be remembered that
some one blundered and the reception resolved itself
largely into a sit-down concert; and concerts are often
tedious, but they border on being unbearable when you
had expected quite another kind of entertainment. This
year we think everyone was pleased with the arrange-
ments. We met our friend Father D’Alton, the now
quite famous historian, who lives in Athenry when he is
at home, and he had much to tell us about the effects,
of that article of ours concerning the two representa-
tives of two ‘‘great’’ London dailies that we struck
against in Athenry whilst they were sniffing about for
outrages. Father D’Alton saw one of them reading
the article. ~We understand the representative of a
“ great”” London daily performed a significant whistle
when he had finished it! Fis Eminence the Cardinal
honoured the reception by his presence, as did also a
number of the Iierarchy. Indeed, it is not too
much to say that His Grace the Archbishop of Tuam’s
aunual reception in Dublin is by a long way the most
important Catholic social function in Ireland.

The report of the first year’s working of the Leinster
College of Irish is most satisfactory reading for Irish
Trelanders. The College has been successful beyond the
hopes of the Provisional Committee, whose estimate of
the probable attendance was exceeded by over one half.
Early in the session it was found necessary to duplicate
both elementary and phonetic classes and make other
additions to the original programme.- The College has
now got into harness for the coming winter, the new
session being formally opened on Friday last by a meet-
ing of Professors, students and friends in the College
premises. An  Cpaoidin  presided and distributed
certificates and diplomas to the successful students. His
Grace the Archbishop of Dublin, to whose sympathetic
interest the project owes much of its success, was also
present, and in a short speech emphasised the neces-
sity of efficiency in Irish teaching methods. The bi-
weekly series of the College classes commenced on Mon-
day, 14th, with a good attendance. The first meeting
of the Saturday classes commences on Saturday, 19th
inst., at 2.30 p.m. Full particulars may be had from
the Registrar.

trict have lost an earnest supporter by the lamented
death of Mr. James Gibney, C.E., which occurred on
Friday last. Mr. Gibney, who was chairman of the

" The Committee of the Greencastle, Belfast, ‘Total
\bstinence Association provided a gramoplione enter-
tainment for its members recently. ~The gramophone,
we are told, emitted selections from the latest English
Music Hall songs. Tt gave the ‘ Freedom of Old
Japan,” ¢ Zuyder Zee,”” ‘¢ Will you lové me in Sep-
tember as you do in May,”* and the choruses were taken
.2p. Why not an Irish gramophone rather than, an
Egglish Music Hall one for the Greencastle Total Ab-
stinence Society? The comic thing is that several of
the prominent men in the Association are members: of
_ the Gaclic League. ~We are sorry to hear that the
Gaclic League is not too strong in Greencastle, but
perhaps one needn’t wonder at that now.

We learn that there is a want in the town of Boyle
for a second Commissioner of Oaths. There were pre-
viously two or three there, but since the deaths of two
no other has been appointed. The only Commissioner
of Oaths at present is the Petty Sessions clerk, who has
several districts to attend to, and as he is often away,
the inconvenience is obvious. And, by the way, when
another Commissioner of Oaths is being appointed, it
would be well—as no doubt there are plenty capable
men to select from—to make a change from the usual
Protestant monopoly and let the second one be a Papist.

New York. We know nothing of this  powerful

On Sunday next, 20th inst., at 3’o'clock, a public

< At this moment more than 160,000 of her children
aro learning Trish ; the National Schools are beginning
to justify their title,” so said General Sir William
Butler in the course of his recent address. The country
may rest assured that so long as he is one of the Com-
missioners Irish-Ireland will have a sturdy champion on
the Board. The recent appointment of six Irish or-
ganisers under the National Education Board is a big
step forward, and what is more, the personnel of the
organisers has met, we believe, with the thorough appro-
bation of the Irish-Ireland public. Mr. Patrick
McSweeney, ‘of Fermoy; Mr. Michael O’Duibhne, of
Spiddal; Mr. Henry Morris, of Dundalk, and Mr.
Michael O’Cleirigh,” of Ballinrobe, are well known
names in the Irish language movement. Mr. William
Falconer, of Belfast, is a new name to us, but we under-
stand he is an excellent man. There is only one lady
among the six new organisers, Miss Mary O’Sullivan,
B.A., from Clare. Miss O’Sullivan, who has had a
distinguished University career, was one of the teach-
ing staff at Loretto Convent, Omagh. We congratu-
late her on receiving such a very responsible appoint-
ment at such a very early age. At Miss O’Sullivan’s
time of life one can freely mention that terrible.word
‘“age’” which one has to be so careful about using in
the presence of more matured ladies. Our readers will
be interested to hear that the only lady organiser of
Irish Language Instruction under the National Board
is a niece of Mr. Michael Sullivan, who has written so
many interesting articles in the LEADER, and who has
done such fine public spirited work in the fight for
Tolerance in the Irish Railway service and in other
connections.

We publish in another column some recent
additions to the Book Selling Department of Messrs.
Clery & Co., Ltd., Dublin. The list includes ¢ Three
Irish Glosses,”” by Whitley Stokes, and ‘ Old Irish
Glosses at Wurzburg and Carlsruhe,’”” which will no
doubt have ‘a special interest for students of the Irish
language.

GENERAL BUTLER ON IRELAND'S MISSION:
"[ T Ireland ceased to be a Catholic country she would
B sink permanently to the level of a mere geographi-
cal expression; she would cease to exist amongst. the

nations, for though we sing ““ A Nation Once Again,”.
and though we have no parliament, we still are a

_ nation, though a poor and tattered one in bondage; if

Treland ceased to be Catholic she would become an
island depending for her importance merely on her
strategical position between America and Europe. It
is well to keep this in mind these days. We were im-
pressed by this conviction vividly when we had heard
General Sir William Butler’s inaugural address to the
Catholic Truth Society of Ireland. The address was
the finest thing we have heard for a long time. Of all
the people in the world we cannot stand—and a great:
many classes of people get on our nerves—the flippant
shallow-minded talker who sets out to generalise on
world affairs is one of the most trying. - But a thinking
man and a man of experience and knowledge and ripe
judgment is extremely interesting on such a topic.
General Butler was equipped for his task. He took his
point of view-—the Catholic point of view—and sur-
veyed the world, and Ireland especially, from the Rock
of Peter. Some men—small men that are too plentiful
in Anglicised Ireland—are so dazed by what little they
know of the outside world that Ireland fades ouf of
their view as an entity. General Butler is in no sense’
a little man, and his survey of the world which he
knows of in other ways than by hearsay, only flings him
back with hope and faith—and charity—to Irish-Ire-
land—a land that must be made a distinctive entity,
and whose mission in the future is to do for the world
what an Trish-Treland of the past did for Europe. %
We often feel impatient at those who argue about
Ireland of the present as if it was the Ireland of
Grattan’s time, the time pre¥ious to the introduction
of machinery in production, previous to railways and
steamships, a time when the relative populations and
wealth of Ireland and Great Britain were utterly.
different. General Butler reminded this country that
“ America and Japan are nearer to us to-day than were
France and Italy of seventy years ago.”’ He touched
on the Yellow Peril. A really far-seeing man knows
that everything of this world is uncertain ; it is only
fools in a tap-room who know all about what is going
to happen 'in a hundred years.. Who knows? We
might yet be glad enough to fight side by side with the
white ‘Britisher . against _the  yéllow Japanese aud ‘i
Chinese invaders at Bantry Bay or Killala! %

Temains of old Irisk. Their work, entitled the ‘‘ The-

- saurus Palweohibernicus,”” which was completed in 1903,

is a monument of erudition.

His association with Ireland, which was a very preg-
nant one, was brought about through the establishment
of the School of Irish Learning in Dublin in 1903 by his
friend, Professor Kuno Meyer. From the outset he
threw himself Leart and soul into its work ; he has been
known to come all the way from Manchester to attend
a meeting of its governors, of which he was one. In his
«capacity as lecturer to the school during the summer
terms from 1903 to 1906, he attracted a band of enthu-
siastic students who will long treasure his memory.
Now for the first time in his career he was in a posi-
tion to lecture on his favourite subject, old Irish,
and those wlko had the good fortune to attend his classes
have never wavered in their enthusiasm for the brilliant
lecturer. Walking to and fro in front of his pupils,
the shy scholar grew wonderfully animated as he ex-
peunded the difficulties of old Irish grammar, and there
was no resisting the gleam of humour which stole over
the delicate face as he dwelt on some particularly subtle
point. It was mainly for the use of students of the
‘School that he drew up the two handbooks, ‘* Selections
from the OId Irish Glosses’’ and ‘‘ Old Irish Para-
digms,”” which, in a measure, have brought the study
of old TIrish within the reach of all.  In conjunction
with Professor Kuno Meyer he edited ‘“ Eriu,”’ the
Journal of the school to which some of his own ripest
work was contributed. He was wont to speak of ‘“ Eriu’’
with undisguised pleasure, and in particular of tke papers
-contributed by students, in the preparation of vhich
he was ever ready to lend counsel and assistance. Quite
recently his' own University of Manchester established
-an honour sckool of Celtic, Professor Strachan taking
charge of the linguistic side and Professor Tout of the
historical. Students attended from Ireland and Wales,
and already the nucleus was laid of a very important
'scheol of research. During tle past year he devoted
himself mainly to Welsh, and when death overtook him
he was putting the finishing touches to a grammar of
Medizval Welsh.

Such, in barest outline, is the work accomplished in
the domain of Celtic by Professor Strachan in a brief
life, and, it should not be forgolten, in the leisure
-snatcked from his duties as Professor of Greek in the
Unitersity of Manchester, a post which he filled since
1885, when he was only 23 years of age. Of the man,
-apart from his work, it would not be in keeping with
his character to speak at any length. Moreover, in a
peculiar degree his work was his life, and he had but
‘few interests outside it. Indifferent to fame and to the
rewards wkich allure most of us, he gave his undivided
-attention to the work to which he was called. Shy and
diffident to an uncommon degree, his gentle, unassum-
ing ways no less than his conspicuous talents endeared
him to all who had the privilege of coming into contact
with him.  To young and old alike he was always

‘courteous and deferential. He loved his students, and
was loved by them in return.
‘happy as when helping or encouraging them in the
-class-room or during one of his favourite rambles
-among the Dublin mountains. Only his friends know
with what high Lopes he looked forward to the progress
of the schools in Dublin and Manchester, and with what
‘pleasure he spoke of the students who showed promise.
‘Of the many sad thoughts which Lis early death calls
forth perhaps one of the saddest is that he was only
beginning to witness the fruition of his arduous labours.
Nowhere will his loss be felt more keenly than in Ire-
land, for the elucidation of whose language he laboured
50 tirelessly and to suck: purpose.

THE IRISH DRAMA AT CORK.

I T was a source of deep disappointment to many that
the Editor of the LEADER was unable to be present
at a performance of the drama,  Eoghan Ruadh
O’Neill,”” during the recent Munster Feis. H_afl‘he
seen it we skould have been treated to a fair criticism
of its merits and shortcomings. As it is the LEADER
has had to depend for its review of the play on one of
those mortals who pass a dreamy existence away up in
the clouds. Nowadays we have a goodly and increasing
number of young men who pose as art critics.  They
appear to be one of the growths—and evil growths at
that—of the new-awakened spirit in the country. They
ars capable of giving forth wlkat would appear to be
learned disquisitions on the right and wrong of various
matters from the point of view of art. They have an
artful, if somewhat inartistic, way of making their
meaning unintelligible to the ordinary reader. And
the latter, poor fellow! rubs his eyes and wonders, and,
with a sigh at his own lack of artistic culture, abandons
the attempt to fathom the mystic words of the critic.

I would not place ‘“ Lee ’’ in the above category. He
can claim tkat he has a practical acquaintance with—
has at least coquetted with—some, if not all, of the
arts. I believe he has done some paintings; to my
knowledge he has also written poetry, and, if I mistake
not, he occasionally conducts choirs. I have not heard
what he has performed in the way of statuary or of
drama, but, judging from his frequent essays on these
subjects in the LEaDER, he may Lave acquired some
knowledge of them. So that ““Lee’ takes not one,
but all, of the arts under his wing. But must not such
all-round acquaintance with them be of necessity of a
superficial character? One would fancy that any single
one of the arts is sufficiently complex to claim the un-
divided attention of one mind, if any kind of excellence
is to be attained, if one is to become a master of the
subject. ~'We have heard of the jack-of-all-trades.
May there not be also such a being as a jack-of-all-arts?

I question the competency of ‘“Lee’’ to assume the
réle of infallible dramatic critic. He takes a very lofty
tone in the manner in which he proceeds to criticise
‘“ Eoghan Ruadh O’Neill.”” He starts out to criticise
the performance, but somehow he gets on the wrong
road, and breaks forth into a rhapsody on sculpture and
on drama generally. Then Le touches on Irish drama
—on what it should be—incidentally bringing in
‘“ Eoghan Ruadh O’Neill.” We must, according to
“ Lee,” follow one narrow path if we are to develop a
national drama. We must take things as we find them;
we must be realists. But we must eschew history—
history that is real and true—and substitute for it
legendary history or romance. Battles and their
accompaniments, though real enough, are abhorrent to
““Lee’s ”’ peaceful and artistic soul.. The mystics wko
dwell in the nebular regions have not yet been per-
turbed by Tennyson’s ‘‘ airy navies grappling in the
central blue.”” So we are to stick to the old mud cabin
with the manure heap and the malodorous duck’s pool
in front, and pigs and bare-legged urchins running in
and out. We are also, I take it, to perpetuate in Irish
what in English drama has come to be termed the
stage Irishman—which ““Lee’’ doubtless has many a
time joined in ridiculing. And we are to Lold it all up
to an admiring audience as being beautiful and artistic

* because, forsooth, it is a subject for pity. Yet one of

the great aims of Irish Ireland is to get rid of these
unpleasant accessories of Trish peasant life, to change
such a condition of things for a better. Presenting
them on the stage, and trying to make people believe

they are beautiful and soul-refreshing things, will cexr-'

tainly not help to get rid of them. ‘‘TLee’’ instances

PREPARING FOR THE HERRINGS,

Y the first week in October the vast shoals of
herrings which form such a vital element in the
life of the dweller on our coasts are well on their way
down the narrow sea which separates us from England.
Then, when his threshing is over and his little tribute
of barley or of oats safely stowed in the granary of the
nearest corn merchant, the seaside peasant sets about
mending his nets, re-tarring his little cot and generally
furbishing up his gear for the coming season, the time
s¢ anxiously expected to redeem the unfulfilled promise
of the harvest.

T happened to be spending the first few days of this
menth in a tiny village on the Wexford coast, and wit-
nessed the assiduous preparations for, and anxious
speculation as to, the approaching herrings. After the
bad summer and the poor harvest I expected to find
the people discouraged and gloomy as to their prospects
from the harvest of the sea, but was agreeably surprised
to find everyone cheerily hopeful. The first man T
spoke to, a tall fellow with tanned face and bushy
whiskers, his keen eyes bent out upon the choppy sea,
told me that the rough winds of early October would
soon blow themselves tired, and then the calm moon-
light nights would come, and a faint mist over the
sea which avould then offer a safe field for the little cots
in which they venture forth upon their quest., There
were rough white waves breaking over the dark blue
now, but the wind would go dowx in a day or two, and
it would be a good season this year, please God. He
had taken his boat out the day before, just prospecting ;
he hadn’t any luck, but he had heard tell of them
getting herrings off Arklow, so they’d soon be-down
here.

There was a bustle of ready-making in the cottage I
put up at that night. All the members of the family
took part in the detailed preparations. The son of the
house, a brawny giant of thirty, with a face fresh as the
earth where the sea beats upon it, sat by the deal table,
cutting cork floats with a huge cobbler’s knife, by the
light of a tallow candle. Iis sister, a thin but vigorous
and genorally pleasant-spoken woman, though sharp in
the tongue at times, was plying a deft needle over the
rapidly disappearing rents in a coarse sail-cloth; a little
boy squatted in the chimney corner, his head bent over
the net he was mending ; while over them all, on a form
by the open hearth, presided the venerable mother of
this seafaring family, a wrinkled, white-haired old

Sitting in that little kitchen, the mingling lights of

peasant folk than a score of Bluebooks can disclose.
They are not much troubled by trawlers in these parts ;
the main difficulby arises from the cots being too small
for any important enterprise.  Frail shells of thirty
- feet in length dare not venture far when there is a bit
of a wind swelling the spring-tide; even the boldest
seldom stay out more than a night at a time, and do not
care to go beyond the three-mile limit, beyond which
the foreign boats seem to have a free field. ~As far as I
could gather it is French smacks get most of the fish
which come down the Channel. My host owned the
whole of his own boat, but the general case was that
several men bought a share in a hoat, with a propor-
tionate share in the working and the profit. ‘‘ Most
of the boats hereabouts are broke up into three, four,
or maybe five shares,”’” he told me. The fishing had
been a bad business enough in this place, but was, they
said, picking up a bit lately. There were nine boats in
this village, and for the last couple of years they had
done better business than the people in the gap a few
miles above them—the ““ Up-gap,”’ as they called it.
The men there could only muster seven boafs. During
the season they used wait till the *‘ Up-gap ’’ fellows
came down, and then the whole' sixteen used start out
together in a little flotilla ; after getting near the shoals
. they would separate and let down their nets at a fair
distance apart. Sometimes one boat would encroach on
the preserves of another; and then fierce disputes
would arise, but they would always be peaceably re-
ferred to the oldest mariner present.
. My host was very proud of his people’s superiority
* -over the ““ Up-gap ’’ men. He told me a quaint story
of how one of them lazy loons, discouraged by last year’s
. bad results, tock his cot and sawed it into two halves;
one of the halves he gave away for firewood, the other
he gave to his wife who, by an ingenious arrangement
of props, turned it into a chicken-house. I could
hardly believe this story, until, next day, I saw the
half-boat, raised a few feet, above the ground, white-
washed on the inside, with cockerels and pullets hopping
cheerfully up and down the steps which let into its
recesses.  Quantum mutatus! The brave little cot which
~ had weathered so many rough-nights and foaming seas,
‘the waves dashing against its brave little bow, now
" become the ignoble, unhonoured domicile of half-a-dozen
scraggily impertinent fowl. This monument of mari-
time inefficiency is still to be seen not a thousand miles
orth of the town of Wexford, but is, I believe and
hope, the only one of the kind. :
The cheery hopefulness, which I have before re-
‘marked, is a very pleasant trait of ourseaside peasantry,
forms one standing contradiction to the much ex-
ited ‘‘ pessimism ’’ of the Irish peasant. The men
t there are a healthy, happy race. The most
igorous among them combine the tilling of the earth
h the searching of the deep. It is refreshing to find
‘even in the overpraised ‘ model’’ .county, some
stock still survives. Long may they

The author of ‘‘ Niav ™ is as interesting as his heroine.

Little way.

It may not be generally known that this question of
spelling had well nigh led to disastrous consequences,
inasmuch as I'ather Peter used to be so harried by
purist. editors so altering his spelling that he had, as is
roported, some serious thoughts of eschewing writing
altogether.  Some of us young fellows up here in

Dublin entertained the laudable desire of maintaining.
a rigid system of orthography, and perhaps it might
have been better if all had conformed to the same
system. What was of greater moment, however, we
had begun to adopt a somewhat affected and imitative
style of writing far removed from the spoken language
and based on the ponderous classic style of Keating.
We were about to march in heavy armour that would
assuredly have crushed the souls out of us.  Father
Peter came along. God bless him for it. ‘‘Cast off
these togs,’’ said he, in that blunt, incisive way of his,
“‘march in your skins if you feel more supple that way.”
He straightway freed us from ourselves; yes, literally
freed modern Irish from the shackles that were being
forged for her, and to-day we are marching boldly for-
ward—in our shirtsleeves. That is his greatest achieve-
ment, not ‘‘ Seadna ’’ nor yet ““Niav’’—one a charm-
ing torso, the other a splendidly-painted canvas of light
and shade, Brian the Nestor somewhat in shade,
Niav herself with a halo, but in mist, Caoilte and his
brother, the doctor, beautifully painted in the fore-
ground, and Prince Murrough of the mighty sword in
brilliant light, with his bosom friend Dulainn Og by his
side—that modest and heroic Dulainn Og who went
dewn at Clontarf in the crimson flood, with his sword
red and a heap of dead around him. Listen to Father
Peter’s description of him at a famous banquet in Kin-
kora: —*¢ he had a heavy load of hair on his
head, falling in locks to his shoulders, jet-black it was,
and the sheen of it was beautiful to see as it shook in
the light to the accompaniment of his speech. Above
his eyes were dark and heavy eye-brows; the eyes of
him were neither too large nor too small, and so steady
were their gaze that, if you looked at them direct, you
would not be wishful to see anger in them. They
appeared to you-as though flame and shade were alter-
nately playing.in them, as the wind and the cloud and
the light play on a hard March day. There was a deep
crease between the eye-brows as in the head of a lion,
and in continuation of this dent was a slender-bridged,
hawklike, large, long nose, a stern mouth below, with
an even row of white glistening teeth which used to
emit, light as he laughed. The beard and moustache
were as jet, and taking into account his hair, his eyes,
the mouth, the teeth, the dent between the brows, you
would see ab a glance what manner of man he was. His
friendship would be of avail to you, but his enmity,
such as you must needs be, far beyond his reach. Men
saw that his head closely resembled that of a lion, and
it was evident to all that he had the vigour, the life, the
strength and the force of a lion in his body and limbs,
and that, if his wrath were roused, he was swift and
deadly as that beast of prey. The Danes knew him
well, but they knew him only as the possessor of the
agility and destructiveness of the raging lion.”’

T have, inaptly perhaps, compared this book to a pic-
ture, but it is more in the nature of a panoramic pro-
cession—the people glide past, and you vow when they
are passed by that you will view them more closely, even

Before deciding whether any particular composition

 Rubayait’’ and ‘“Maud.” No definition can be

First as to form. To speak poetically means to speak
in such a manner that we cannot imagine what we have
said could ever be said differently. In a word, so to
express our meaning that the expression seems the only
natural and inevitable expression of that particular
idea. A single line of poetry may make a cameo by
itself. Thus when the poet says:—

To wander in youth by a maiden’s side,”

LORD ASHTOWN BEFORE THE CONGESTED
DISTRICTS COMMISSION.

HE habits of the “‘carrion-crow’” are not the habits
of good society ; but I am not here dealing with the
““carrion-crow,”’ but with inferior beef produced under
Lord Ashtown’s ranch system. Lord Ashtown, flushed
with the Glenaheiry “‘victory’’( ?), next appears before the
Congested Districts Commission, sitting at Ballinasloe,
in the character of a crusader against all those foolish
scientific agriculturists, who advocate early maturity in
live stock—‘‘ baby >’ beef, and such things. Professor
Campbell and his co-workers of the Department of
Agriculture are engaged raising ‘‘ baby ” beef, on the
experiment farm, at Athenry; but Lord Ashtown has
his eye on Professor Campbell, as the ‘ Skibbereen
Bagle ” had its eye on the Czar of Russia, and he says
in effect, if not in words, ‘‘ away with Professor Camp-
bell and his ¢ baby ’ beef, he’s not the man for Galway.
Give us still our old nobility, the Connaught rancher
and the long-horned cattle, to ‘ lump’ into beef, at 4
to 4} year-old.” It will be noted that it is not so much
a question of the carrion-crow hovering over theranches,
but of the inferior beef that is raised on them. Thecawing
of the carrion-crow is not of much account, it has become
monotonous, no one minds it, and it will pass
away; but it is a serious consideration for the people of
the United Kingdom if the soil of this country is to be
wasted any longer in the production of inferior beef. I
appeal to Leinster graziers, who buy gaunt stores off
the Connaught ranches, to tell us how many of those
cattle have they found it impossible to fatten? Of the
twenty, thirty, or forty 3 to 34 year-old cattle, bought
in lots at the October fair of Ballinasloe, or the May
fair of Loughrea, how many have twrned out failures
when put to fatten on the Leinster pastures?  What
numbers of them have gone to the class of ““rough and
unfinished,”” which glut the beef markets from the
middle of September to the middle of November each
yoar?! No matter, says Lord Ashtown, the game
must go on, the Leinster big grazier. wants out-
lying, strong 3 year-old cattle, wherewith to stock his
pastures, and we, Connaught ranchers—gentlemen-
jobbers—will supply him with big lots of same, half the
units of which he will not see in the hurry of the fair.
Is Lord Ashtown really aware how ridiculous he -
makes himself in advocating methods of agriculture
long since played out—systems wholly at variance with
present day scientific farming? Tt is a startling thing
to find a nobleman, who, as a resident proprietor, farms
a big oxtent of land in Galway, asserting that the feed-
ing of cattle, for two or three years, on unsheltered
coarse pasbure, is the most profitable agricultural
system to follow in Connaught. Why, the thing goes
bhack to the time of Arthur Young (1776-79), it is the
feeding of one horned beast to two or three Irish acres
cach year, and a miserable return therefrom in the way
of rent. Lord Ashtown is the'Rip Van Winkle, ob-
livious of the changes which have taken place since the
days of Arthur Young. There was a time when the
T.einster big grazier used be getting rich, like the drone,*
in his sleep ; it was when his fat cattle, even the ‘‘rough

Irise MonEY AND ENGLISH SECUR:ITIES.

Dear Sir—Commenting in your last issue on the
fact that the Waterford City and County Infirmary
have some funds to invest, and that they propose in-
vesting them in English railways, etc., you ask the
question: ‘“ Are the preference shares of Irish railways
not equal to those of English railways?”’

The Board of Trade returns on.the workings of the
railways in the United Kingdom for the year 1906 sup-
plies an answer to your question. The proportion of
net earnings to capital invested in the railways of the
United Kingdom for the year 1906 was 3.45 per cent.,
and the proportion of net earnings to capital invested
in railways in Ireland was 3.75 per cent. I think the
Waterford County and City Infirmary should consider
““ Bog stock > a good enough security for their money.
Particularly so, seeing that the net earnings of the Irish
railways for the year 1906 have increased by £65,749
over the net earnings of 1905.—Yours truly,

““ RarLways.”
